articles.title: The State of React and the Community in 2025
articles.description: React continues to be a major dependency in the JavaScript world but recent innovations have led to much discussion about how it should move forward. Redux maintainer Mark Erikson gives an overview of React’s development over time, what led to some of its innovations, and dispels some ‘FUD and confusion’ about where it's headed.
articles.author: Mark Erikson

articles.title: Announcing Oxlint 1.0: The Super Fast Linter
articles.description: First appearing just 18 months ago, Oxlint has made an impact by being an incredibly fast Rust-powered linter for JavaScript and TypeScript, boasting a 50~100x performance improvement over ESLint while still having support for hundreds of its rules. Now, it’s gone stable.
articles.author: Boshen Chen and Cameron Clark

articles.title: pnpm 10.12 Introduces an Experimental Global Virtual Store
articles.description: pnpm has long been prized for its speed and efficiency over npm. v10.12 takes things further introducing a ‘global virtual store’ that node_modules symlinks to, enabling projects to share dependencies without them being installed numerous times.
articles.author: Sarah Gooding (Socket)

articles.title: Suppressions of Suppressions
articles.description: If you’re using a linter to keep your code clean, you may have silenced rules that feel too strict or irrelevant. But those suppressions can bury serious bugs. Dan Abramov argues for adding a rule to forbid disabling your most critical checks.
articles.author: Dan Abramov

articles.title: How JavaScript Was Written Back in the Day
articles.description: It’s not the earliest JavaScript, but Trevor looks back at code written in the 2006-2015 pre-ES6 era.
articles.author: Trevor I. Lasn

articles.title: Native Hot Module Reloading in Node via Module Hooks
articles.description: A clever use of module hooks to implement ‘hot module’ functionality natively and efficiently.
articles.author: Immaculata

articles.title: npmgraph: A Tool to Visualize npm Module Dependencies
articles.description: Give this Web-based tool one or more npm package names (or even your package.json file) and you can see a visualization of the dependency graphs for those packages, including where they intersect. Packages can be colored by various criteria (such as number of maintainers) and you can download SVGs of the graphs.
articles.author: Kieffer, Brigante, et al.

articles.title: Jest 30: Faster, Leaner, and Better JS Testing
articles.description: A ‘noticeably faster’ release of this popular testing framework, adding improved ESM and TypeScript support, performance improvements and more. Upgrade docs here.
articles.author: Zaytsev & Nakazawa

articles.title: 🍊 Orange ORM: An Active Record ORM for JavaScript and TypeScript
articles.description: A powerful ORM for Node, Bun and Deno, supporting both TypeScript and JavaScript, and both CommonJS and ESM. It follows an Active Record-style querying approach, is well documented, and certainly worth a look if working with most of the popular SQL databases.
articles.author: Lars-Erik Roald

articles.title: Vue Equipment: A Toolkit for Nuxt and Vue.js
articles.description: A collection of ready-to-use plugins and composables for building modern web apps with both Vue and Nuxt. Explained here.
articles.author: Magic as a Service

articles.title: 🌓 DarkModeJS 2.0: A Utility Package for Managing Dark Mode
articles.description: Uses the matchMedia API and the prefers-color-scheme media query to fire functions whenever a user is in dark mode (or changes the mode).
articles.author: Assortment

articles.title: 🕹️ Odyc.js: A JS Library for Pixel Games/Stories
articles.description: Has a bit of a 8-bit Game Boy Color vibe to it. You can create games, and try some examples, in this online playground.
articles.author: Charles Cailleteau

articles.description: Surfin' Safari? Apple's WWDC25 event this week saw numerous developments that aren't 'liquid glass' related, including a look at what's new in Safari 26 (beta) including support for pattern modifiers in RegExp objects.
A writeup of a vulnerability found in OpenPGP.js.
Matteo Collina gives us a public service announcement around the end-of-life of some recent Node.js versions and some tips on upgrading to v22.
Gleam is an easy to read/write language targeting both Erlang and JS runtimes. The big news is that Gleam compiled to JS is now 30% faster.
H3 v2 Beta – Cross-runtime Web standards focused HTTP server framework.
Node.js v24.2 (Current) – import.meta.main is a new boolean value available in ES modules that tells you if the current module was the entry point of the current process.
Visual Studio Code May 2025 – Big enhancements to MCP support, but VS Code extensions can now also use ES modules.
Deno 2.3.6, Rollup v4.43, Jasmine 5.8, Vue DevTools v7 for Firefox

articles.title: A New Way to Style Gaps in CSS
articles.description: The Microsoft Edge team shares an update on the work underway to implement gap decorations, a welcome addition that should do away with the need for various pseudo-element hacks. If you want to play around with things, there’s an interactive demo page showcasing what’s possible (Note: it’s behind a flag in Chromium-based browsers).
articles.author: Omekara and Brosset

articles.title: ✅ You're Not a Front-End Developer Until You've...
articles.description: A fun, tongue-in-cheek checklist of the various oddities we all do as frontend devs. We’ve shared Nic’s site before — it’s one you’ll no doubt poke around and have fun with.
articles.author: Nic Chan

articles.title: Payload is Joining Figma
articles.description: The headless Next.js-native CMS Payload has been acquired by design giant Figma. It will remain open-source but can now “integrate with design systems in ways no other CMS can”. James Mikrut went on Syntax to chat with Wes Bos about the news.
articles.author: James Mikrut

articles.title: HTML Spec Change: Escaping < and > in Attributes
articles.description: The Chrome team tells us about an interesting recent change to the HTML specification around how angled brackets are escaped in attributes. It’s designed to help prevent mutation XSS vulnerabilities.
articles.author: Michał Bentkowski

articles.title: Animating Zooming using CSS: Transform Order is Important… Sometimes
articles.description: Jakes notes how CSS uses a rather complex algorithm to decide how to approach animating transforms. He warns that if we get it wrong, you  end up with an 'unnatural' animation. As such, here’s how it works (complete with feline demos), and how to avoid the potential pitfalls.
articles.author: Jake Archibald

articles.title: What I Wish Someone Told Me When I Was Getting Into ARIA
articles.description: Eric shares what you need to know about ‘Accessible Rich Internet Applications’ in this approachable post, providing some guiding thoughts and debunking some common misconceptions too. A good place to pad out your understanding of all things ARIA.
articles.author: Eric Bailey

articles.title: Safari at WWDC '25: The Ghost of Christmas Past
articles.description: Now that the dust has settled on Apple’s various announcements made last week at their annual conference, Alex shares a rather critical analysis of where things stand on the browser/web front, accusing Apple of “gaslighting” and “suppressing the web”.
articles.author: Alex Russell

articles.title: How to Keep Up With New CSS Features
articles.description: Resources worth knowing about for keeping on top of CSS features, beyond just aimless Googling.
articles.author: Sacha Greif

articles.title: Breaking Boundaries: Building a Tangram Puzzle With (S)CSS
articles.description: An interesting experiment in seeing just how far CSS can be pushed when creating interactive user interfaces.
articles.author: Sladjana Stojanovic

articles.title: 1fr 1fr vs auto auto vs 50% 50%
articles.description: You’d be forgiven that thinking these properties may do the same thing when used to create some columns, but Chris highlights the subtle differences in output.
articles.author: Chris Coyier

articles.title: The Hamburger-Menu Icon Today: Is it Recognizable?
articles.description: After being with us for more than a decade, the hamburger menu is now an established, recognizable pattern, but “familiarity alone doesn’t erase its fundamental tradeoffs”.
articles.author: Kate Kaplan

articles.title: Anime.js 4.0: A JS Animation Library for the Web
articles.description: If you’re somewhat fatigued when it comes to working with web animations, Anime.js may well refresh your appetite. This mature library recently saw a major upgrade — it's a solid option for animating CSS properties, SVGs, the DOM, and JS objects. It’s well-built, and now has fresh documentation to help you get started.
articles.author: Julian Garner

articles.title: Anchors: Tailwind Plugin That Provides a Simple API for Working with CSS Anchor Positioning
articles.description: The Anchor Positioning API is not quite at full support yet in browsers, but if you’re interested in trying it out in a Tailwind project, this will help.
articles.author: Toolwind

articles.title: Critical CSS Generator: Web-based Tool to Extract Critical CSS from Any URL
articles.description: Enter a URL along with viewport width/height along with render wait time and this tool will generate the minified CSS. The page also includes instructions on how to add it to your page.
articles.author: Kigo

articles.title: DarkModeJS 2.0: A Utility Package for Managing Dark Mode
articles.description: Uses the matchMedia API and the prefers-color-scheme media query to fire functions whenever a user is in dark mode (or changes the mode).
articles.author: Assortment

articles.title: RampenSau: A Color Palette Generation Library Using Hue Cycling and Easing Functions
articles.description: You can try out an implementation on the home page, which allows you to adjust numerous settings to show the capabilities of the library.
articles.author: David Aerne
